title: Sunday, February 11
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: February 11, 2018
Preacher: Broderick Greer

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This Sunday fell during Ordinary Time after Epiphany, specifically the Last Sunday after Epiphany (Transfiguration Sunday in many traditions). The Revised Common Lectionary readings would have included 2 Kings 2:1-12 (Elijah's ascension), Psalm 50:1-6, 2 Corinthians 4:3-6, and Mark 9:2-9 (the Transfiguration narrative). The liturgical season emphasized Christ's revealed glory and the disciples' transformative encounter with the divine before moving toward Lenten preparation.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a period of relative stability following the 2015 General Convention's formal recognition of marriage equality. Bishop Robert Wright had been leading the Diocese of Colorado since 2013 and was focused on missional renewal across the Rocky Mountain region. No major denominational controversies or episcopal elections were occurring in early February 2018.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver experienced typical February winter weather with fluctuating temperatures and periodic snow. Local political attention was focused on the 2018 midterm election cycle, with Colorado Democrats and Republicans preparing campaign strategies. The opioid crisis remained a pressing public health concern in Denver and statewide. Downtown Denver and the Capitol Hill neighborhood (where Saint John's Cathedral is located) were experiencing continued gentrification and real estate development pressure.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration was in its second year; immigration policy and the status of DACA (Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals) dominated national debate following failed negotiations in Congress the previous week. The #MeToo movement continued to generate major news as high-profile sexual misconduct allegations unfolded weekly across industries. The Winter Olympics in PyeongChang, South Korea were occurring (Feb 9-25), capturing significant American media attention. Gun violence remained a national flashpoint following the Parkland, Florida school shooting exactly one week earlier (February 14 was still three days away, but the shooting's immediate aftermath was dominating news cycles).

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

North and South Korea were engaged in diplomatic engagement surrounding the PyeongChang Olympics, with joint teams marching together and preliminary talks about denuclearization. The Syrian civil war continued with ongoing Russian and Iranian military involvement supporting the Assad regime. Venezuela's humanitarian and political crisis was deepening under Nicolás Maduro's authoritarian governance, with widespread shortages and refugee outflows accelerating.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood reflected deep political polarization and anxiety about social fractures, amplified by the emerging revelations of sexual harassment and abuse across American institutions. The country was grappling with questions about safety, accountability, and institutional trust. The PyeongChang Olympics provided a rare moment of international optimism and athletic spectacle. In Denver specifically, a proud sports culture was present (the Broncos had finished their 2017 season in January), and the city maintained its identity as a progressive mountain metropolis amid national divisions.
